description: This figure presents Thermo-Calc calculated thermodynamic equilibrium results for the Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel system. Subplot (a) is a pseudo-binary phase diagram (wt.% C vs temperature) illustrating phase regions (L, L+δ, δ+γ, γ, γ+κ, δ+γ+κ), peritectic reaction, and eutectoid reaction at 851 °C, marked for the target 0.8 wt.% C alloy. Subplot (b) plots temperature-dependent volume fractions of δ-ferrite, γ-austenite matrix, ordered B2 intermetallic, and κ-carbide, identifying the δ to B2 order-disorder transition at 1125 °C and low κ-carbide fraction. Subplots (c) and (d) show equilibrium solute (Mn, Al, Ni, C) concentrations in γ matrix and B2 phase respectively, confirming Al, Ni, Mn enrichment and negligible C solubility in B2, with solute partitioning from γ to B2 occurring as temperature decreases. These results support phase evolution predictions during thermomechanical processing and annealing for the alloy.

This figure presents the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel under different processing conditions, with all subgraphs sharing a 20 μm scale bar. Subgraph (a) is an optical micrograph of the as-cast sample, showing δ-ferrite dendrites distributed in the γ-austenite matrix. Subgraphs (b) and (c) are optical micrographs of hot-rolled (HR) and cold-rolled (CR) samples separately, demonstrating that δ-ferrite transforms into lamellar B2 stringer bands after HR, and the B2 bands are further thinned with γ grains flattened into pancake shape after CR. Subgraph (d) is a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) image of the HR sample, exhibiting intragranular B2 (IG-B2) precipitates nucleated at shear bands within the γ matrix. These observations confirm that thermomechanical processing can effectively regulate the phase morphology and distribution of the studied steel, laying a microstructural foundation for optimizing its mechanical properties.

This group of field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images characterizes the microstructure of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel annealed at 900 °C for different durations: subfigures a/d/g correspond to sample A5 (annealed for 5 min), b/e/h to A15 (15 min), and c/f/i to A30 (30 min). Lower-magnification images (a/b/c) show parallel B2 stringer bands and pancake-shaped unrecrystallized austenite (URE-γ). Medium (d/e/f) and higher-magnification (g/h/i) images reveal fine needle-like intragranular B2 precipitates (IG-B2) inside URE-γ grains, and coarse intergranular B2 precipitates (GB-B2) at austenite grain boundaries. As annealing time increases, IG-B2 gradually coarsens, and GB-B2 size also increases, while URE-γ maintains its elongated morphology. This figure demonstrates the evolution of three B2 phase morphologies (stringer bands, IG-B2, GB-B2) with annealing time, providing microstructural evidence for tuning precipitation strengthening and mechanical properties of the steel.

This figure characterizes the microstructure and elemental distribution of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel sample A30 (annealed at 900 °C for 30 min after cold rolling). Subfigure (a) is a bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image with a 5 μm scale bar, revealing irregular grain boundary B2 precipitates (GB-B2), needle-shaped intragranular B2 precipitates (IG-B2), and annealing twins in the austenite matrix; its inset demonstrates B2 precipitation at annealing twins. Subfigures (b-f) are TEM-energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental maps for Fe (b), Mn (c), C (d), Al (e), and Ni (f), respectively. EDS results confirm that the B2 intermetallic phase is enriched in Fe, Al, and Ni, while depleted in Mn and C, verifying its chemical formula as Fe(Ni,Mn)Al with negligible C solubility. This figure supports the conclusion that annealing twins act as heterogeneous nucleation sites for B2 precipitates, which contributes to precipitation strengthening of the alloy.

This figure presents mechanical properties and corresponding microstructural failure mechanisms of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel processed by different routes: hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR), and 900 °C annealing for 5 min (A5), 10 min (A10), 15 min (A15), and 30 min (A30). Subfigure (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves: CR sample exhibits the highest tensile strength ~1500 MPa but the lowest ductility and toughness (0.11 kJ/mol, 15.2 J/cm³); HR sample has ~1000 MPa strength, ~6.8% elongation, toughness 0.48 kJ/mol (66.5 J/cm³); annealed samples show improved ductility and toughness with increasing annealing time, with A30 achieving the highest toughness of 0.89 kJ/mol (123.3 J/cm³) with ~750 MPa strength and ~20% elongation. Subfigures (b-e) are SEM and optical micrographs of deformed tensile gauges: (b) A5 shows B2 stringer de-cohesion; (c) A10 presents micro-cracks formed in B2 stringers perpendicular to loading direction; (d) A15 exhibits void nucleation at B2 stringer boundaries; (e) A30 shows B2 stringer de-cohesion and micro-crack coalescence. Subfigure (f) is an EBSD orientation map of A30, revealing recrystallized bamboo-like B2 stringers with distinct grain boundaries, which are primary crack initiation sites. The figure demonstrates that B2 intermetallic stringer de-cohesion, void nucleation and micro-crack propagation are dominant failure mechanisms, and annealing modulates B2 stringer morphology to optimize strength-ductility-toughness balance.

This figure illustrates mechanical properties and corresponding deformed microstructures of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel processed by modified high-speed hot rolling, cold rolling, followed by 900 °C annealing for 5 min (A5), 15 min (A15), 30 min (A30), plus hot-rolled (HR) and cold-rolled (CR) reference samples. Subplot (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves: CR sample exhibits the highest tensile strength ~1650 MPa with nearly no ductility, HR sample shows ~900 MPa strength and ~2% ductility, while annealed samples show decreasing strength (A5: ~1200 MPa, A15: ~970 MPa, A30: ~870 MPa) and increasing elongation (A5: ~20%, A15: ~24%, A30: ~30%) with extended annealing time, with toughness values of 1.66 kJ/mol (230.06 J/cm³), 1.56 kJ/mol (216.32 J/cm³), 1.72 kJ/mol (238.38 J/cm³) for A5, A15, A30 respectively. Subplot (b) is Holloman's plot (lnσ vs lnε) presenting two-stage strain hardening behavior, with initial low strain hardening exponents (n=0.069 for A5, 0.064 for A15, 0.054 for A30) and higher steady-state n values (0.179 for A5, 0.25 for A15, 0.27 for A30). Subplots (c)-(e) are optical micrographs (50 μm scale bar) of deformed tensile gauge sections, showing aligned B2 intermetallic stringer bands and microcracks near fracture surfaces, with A30 showing reduced crack susceptibility. The figure demonstrates that optimized annealing process refines B2 stringers, improves austenite plastic deformation capacity, and achieves excellent strength-ductility-toughness balance suitable for automotive lightweight applications.

This figure characterizes the microstructural deformation behavior of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel under cold rolling (CR) and interrupted tensile deformation at engineering strain e=0.05. It contains 5 subgraphs: (a) EBSD phase map of CR sample, showing co-deformation of deformed γ matrix and deformed B2 intermetallic stringers; (b) parallel EBSD KAM (Kernel Average Misorientation, value range 0-5, proportional to dislocation density) map of CR sample, revealing higher strain localization in softer γ phase than harder B2 phase. (c) TEM bright-field image of A15 sample (annealed at 900℃ for 15 min) after interrupted tensile test, showing morphologies of deformed B2 and γ phases. (d) EBSD phase map of post-tensile A15 sample, identifying B2 precipitates, deformed γ phase, and γ grain boundaries; (e) corresponding KAM map of post-tensile A15 sample, demonstrating geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) localization at γ/B2 interfaces. The results confirm that strain partitioning between γ and B2 phases leads to strain accumulation at phase boundaries, supporting that phase interface dislocation blocking contributes to the measured strain hardening exponent of 0.19-0.27 for the alloy.
